q: Which combination of image quality labels is allowed?
opts: Good and Blurry
Dark and Blurry
Good and Dark
Good, Dark, and Blurry
explain: 'Dark' and 'Blurry' can be selected together, but 'Good' cannot be combined with either 'Dark' or 'Blurry'. If 'Good' is selected, the other two are disabled, and vice versa.

q: What is the correct rule when both 'Dark' and 'Blurry' apply to an image?
opts: Select only the more severe of the two
Select both 'Dark' and 'Blurry'; do not select 'Good'
Select 'Good' since the overall quality is poor
Leave Image Quality blank and note it in Additional Information
explain: Image Quality is multi-select. 'Dark' and 'Blurry' can be selected together. However, 'Good' is mutually exclusive with both — if the image has any quality issue, 'Good' must not be selected.

q: An image shows an extreme close-up of a small lesion that fills most of the frame. Which distance category should be selected?
opts: distance-part-plant
distance-whole-plant
distance-macro
distance-multiple-plants
explain: 'distance-macro' is the correct label for extreme close-ups of very small targets — such as a spot, lesion, insect, or egg cluster — that fill most of the frame.

q: A photo shows mostly bare soil with a small portion of the plant visible. Which subject type applies?
opts: subject-crop
subject-invalid
subject-soil
distance-field
explain: 'subject-soil' applies when the camera is pointed mostly at the ground and the plant is limited to a minority of the frame.

q: An image shows a person's hand holding a plant with visible pest damage. Which image type label must be selected?
opts: subject-crop
subject-soil
subject-invalid
PII
explain: When a person is present in the image, PII (Personally Identifiable Information) must be selected — regardless of whether a crop is also visible. Hands, people, or any personal identifiers in the frame require the PII label.

q: Which image type label should be applied if an image contains someone's GPS coordinates or an ID card photo?
opts: subject-invalid
PII
distance-field
subject-soil
explain: PII (Personally Identifiable Information) must be selected if the image contains lat-long coordinates, an ID card photograph, car number plate, or any other sensitive personal information — even if a crop is also visible.

q: What should an annotator do if the crop in an image is not listed in the crop name options?
opts: Leave the field blank
Select the most similar crop
Select 'Others' and enter the crop name in the text box
Flag the image and skip annotation
explain: Select 'Others' — a text box will appear where you enter the crop name. The options list will be updated based on this feedback.

q: Which issue type should be selected when damage caused by a pest is visible but the pest itself is absent?
opts: Pest
Disease
Pest Symptom
Weed
explain: 'Pest Symptom' covers visible damage caused by a pest (holes in leaves, frass, feeding marks) even when the pest itself is not currently visible in the image.

q: If a crop is completely healthy with no visible issues, which combination is correct?
opts: Leave Issue Type blank; write 'Healthy' in Additional Information
Select 'None (Healthy)' in Issue Type; leave Issue Name blank
Select 'None (Healthy)' in Issue Type and Issue Name
Select 'Good' in Image Quality and skip Issue fields
explain: Select 'None (Healthy)' in Issue Type, and leave Issue Name blank. Issue Type and Issue fields are mutually exclusive with 'None (Healthy)'.

q: Multiple issue types can be selected simultaneously when more than one is present in an image.
opts: True
False
explain: True. The Issue Type field is multi-select — if a pest and a disease are both visible, both should be selected.

q: When should separate bounding boxes be drawn instead of one combined box?
opts: When issues are on different crops
When issues are far apart from each other
When there are more than two issue types
When the image is blurry
explain: Multiple issues can be covered in a single bounding box as long as it doesn't cover the whole image. If issues are far apart, draw individual bounding boxes for each.

q: What should a bounding box enclose?
opts: The entire plant
The entire image frame
The pest, visible lifecycle stage, and damaged part of the plant
Only the pest or insect body
explain: Draw the bounding box around the specific region of interest — this includes the pest itself, the visible lifecycle stage, and the damaged part of the plant.

q: For a disease issue, what are the three stage options available in the Issue Stage field?
opts: Onset, Progression, Severe
Early, Medium, Late
Mild, Moderate, Critical
Initial, Active, Terminal
explain: For disease issues, the three Issue Stage options defined in the protocol are Early, Medium, and Late.

q: When annotating an image with multiple instances of the same issue, how should Issue Stage be handled?
opts: Fill Issue Stage once per issue type
Leave Issue Stage blank when the same issue appears more than once
Select the stage for each instance, even if it is the same issue appearing multiple times
Select only the most severe stage observed
explain: Issue Stage must be selected for each issue found in the image, even when there are multiple instances of the same issue. Every occurrence must be individually staged.

q: Which statement about the Issue Name field is correct?
opts: It is a separate field for each issue type (pests, diseases, weeds each have their own)
It is a single unified field combining pest names, disease names, deficiency types, and weed names
It only appears when 'Pest' or 'Disease' is selected in Issue Type
It is optional and can be left blank if Issue Type is filled
explain: Issue Name is a single unified field that combines all issue categories — pest names, disease names, nutrient deficiency types, and weed names — into one list. The options shown depend on what was selected in Issue Type.

q: What is the correct approach when an annotator selects 'Others' in the Issue Name field?
opts: Leave the text box blank and flag it to the coordinator
Use only standardised names from the approved list — 'Others' cannot be selected
Write the exact name of the issue in the text box that appears alongside the 'Others' option
Skip Issue Stage and Issue Region for that bounding box
explain: When 'Others' is selected in Issue Name, a text box appears immediately below or beside it. The annotator must write the exact name of the issue there. This also applies when the name is not available in the options list.

q: Which fields must always be filled together whenever any issue exists in an image?
opts: Image Quality and Image Type
Issue Type, Issue Name, and Issue Region
Bounding Box and Additional Information
Issue Stage and Crop Name
explain: Issue Region must be filled in conjunction with Issue Type and Issue Name. If any issue exists, the affected plant part must also be identified — these three fields are interdependent.

q: Which of these should be noted in the Additional Information field?
opts: The crop name selected
The number of bounding boxes drawn
An image that appears to be a screenshot rather than a direct capture
The annotator's name
explain: Additional Information should capture unusual observations: watermarks, screenshots, multiple crops in one frame, or obstructions. Standard annotation data (crop name, bounding boxes) belongs in the dedicated fields.

q: When is the Additional Information field mandatory?
opts: Always — it must be filled for every image
Only when 'Others' is selected in Issue Name
It is never mandatory but should always be filled if anything unusual is observed
Only when the image quality is Dark or Blurry
explain: Additional Information is marked as not mandatory (No) in the protocol. However, annotators must always fill it if anything unusual is observed — even if it seems minor.

q: 'Issue Name' should be left blank only when which condition is met?
opts: The image quality is Dark or Blurry
Issue Type is 'None (Healthy)'
The crop is not listed in the options
The image contains PII
explain: Leave Issue Name blank only if Issue Type = None (Healthy). In all other cases, specific issue names must be selected for every issue present.

q: At what zoom level should images be captured according to the protocol?
opts: 2x for better detail
Default 1x zoom
Any zoom level as long as the issue is visible
3x or higher to fill the frame with the affected area
explain: Images must be captured at the default 1x zoom level. Changing the zoom introduces inconsistencies that make it harder for the model to learn correctly.

q: In which orientation should all crop images be captured?
opts: Landscape (phone held horizontally)
Portrait (phone held vertically)
Either orientation is acceptable
Depends on the crop being photographed
explain: All images must be captured in portrait format — holding the mobile phone vertically. This is a mandatory requirement of the image capture protocol.

q: Why should flash or artificial lighting never be used when capturing crop images?
opts: It drains the phone battery too quickly
It creates glare, shadows, and unnatural colour tones that can distort visual symptoms of pests and diseases
It makes the image file size too large for upload
It overexposes the image and makes annotation harder
explain: Flash creates glare, shadows, and unnatural colour tones which distort the key visual symptoms of pests and diseases. Images should always be captured in natural daylight.

q: What should a user do if lighting conditions in the field are poor?
opts: Use the phone torch to illuminate the affected area
Switch to a higher ISO setting in the camera
Wait for better lighting conditions rather than using flash or artificial light
Capture the image anyway and flag it in Additional Information
explain: The protocol is clear: if lighting is poor, wait for better conditions. Using flash or artificial lighting distorts visual symptoms and reduces image quality for model training.

q: Why are long-shot images discouraged even when an agricultural expert can identify the issue from them?
opts: Long-shot images take up more storage space
They cause intra-class variability that biases the model toward predicting all long-shot images as a single class
Long-shot images cannot be annotated with bounding boxes
They are automatically rejected by the upload system
explain: Intra-class variability between long and close-shot images causes the model to become biased — for example, predicting all long-shot images as Brown Plant Hopper regardless of the actual issue. Close-shot images are always required.

q: Why should manual camera settings like ISO, focus, and exposure be avoided?
opts: They are not available on most mobile phones
Manually changing settings introduces inconsistencies in image quality that make it harder for the model to learn correctly
They increase image file size beyond the upload limit
They cause the camera app to crash on older devices
explain: Manually adjusting settings like ISO, focus, or exposure introduces inconsistencies across images, which disrupts model training. The camera should always be left on its default auto settings.

q: What is the effect of having too many leaves, plants, or background distractions in a single image?
opts: It improves dataset diversity
It allows the model to learn from a wider context
It introduces noisy artefacts that allow the model to capture biases rather than focusing on the actual issue
It has no effect on model training
explain: The presence of noisy artefacts and irrelevant background elements causes the model to capture nuanced biases rather than focusing on the highlighted issue. The affected plant part should fill most of the frame.

q: Why should images not be captured in burst mode?
opts: Burst mode reduces image resolution
Burst mode captures 5–10 nearly identical frames, resulting in duplicate images that will be rejected by the de-duplication model
Burst mode uses more mobile data during upload
Burst mode is not supported by the annotation tool
explain: Burst mode captures multiple nearly identical frames in a second, creating duplicate images. The image de-duplication model will reject these, reducing the effective dataset size.

q: What is the maximum recommended number of images to capture per scene?
opts: One image per scene
No more than two images per scene
Up to five images per scene
As many as needed to document the issue fully
explain: The protocol recommends capturing no more than two images per scene. The de-duplication model will reject multiple images from the same scene as duplicates.

q: Which of the following qualifies as a duplicate image?
opts: Two images of different leaves on the same plant
Two images of the same leaf taken from different angles on different days
Multiple shots of the same leaf from the same angle and position with no visible difference
Images of the same crop variety taken on different farms
explain: Duplicate images are photos that look the same or nearly the same — taken from the same angle, of the same leaf or area, with little to no visible difference. This includes burst-mode captures and re-clicks of the same shot.

q: Why should different life stages of pests and symptoms be captured?
opts: To meet a minimum image count requirement
To build a more comprehensive dataset that improves the model's ability to detect issues across various stages
To reduce the number of annotation labels needed
Life stage does not affect model training
explain: Capturing different life stages of crops, pests, and symptoms helps build a more comprehensive dataset, improving the model's ability to detect issues accurately across various stages of development.

q: Which pest life stages should be prioritised when capturing images?
opts: The most recently observed stage only
The adult stage only, as it is easiest to identify
Harmful pest life stages should be prioritised
All life stages are equally important; no prioritisation needed
explain: The protocol explicitly states that harmful pest life stages should be prioritised when capturing images, as these are most critical for model training and field diagnosis.

q: Why should old or recycled images from the phone gallery not be submitted?
opts: They are usually lower resolution than fresh captures
Old images may not represent the current crop condition and reduce the reliability of the dataset
The upload system can detect and block gallery images automatically
Gallery images are typically in the wrong file format
explain: Old images may not reflect the current crop condition. The protocol requires fresh images captured directly from the field visit to ensure the dataset is accurate and up to date.

q: Why should watermarks on images be avoided?
opts: Watermarks increase file size beyond upload limits
Watermarks can obstruct important visual details and interfere with model training or manual annotation
Watermarks make the image appear blurry
The annotation tool cannot process images with watermarks
explain: Watermarks (brand logos, device names, timestamps) obstruct key visual details in the image and interfere with both manual annotation and model training. Watermark settings should be turned off in the camera app before capturing.

q: What is the correct practice to avoid cluttered images?
opts: Use a wider shot to show the full context of the affected area
Centre the image on the affected plant part and minimise surrounding noise like soil, tools, and other plants
Capture the image from above to reduce background distractions
Include nearby plants to provide context for the model
explain: The image should be centred on the affected plant part with minimal surrounding noise. Hands, tools, human shadows, and irrelevant background elements should all be excluded from the frame.

q: Why should images not be captured from too far away?
opts: Wide shots consume more storage space
Symptoms or pests may become unclear or lost in wider shots, making annotation and model training harder
The annotation tool does not support long-shot images
Far-away images are automatically classified as duplicates
explain: Capturing images from too far away makes symptoms and pests unclear or invisible. The main issue should fill a large part of the frame. Users should move closer to the affected leaf, stem, or fruit.
